source: ILO Just Transition Guidelines 2015 / COP26 / World Bank

question: Under Australian law (as an example of best-practice Indigenous Cultural Heritage protection), the Native Title Act 1993 and Heritage Protection Acts require mining companies to take which actions before disturbing ground with potential Indigenous cultural heritage significance?

options.A: Post a notice on the company website inviting Indigenous groups to identify culturally sensitive areas within 30 days
options.B: Engage an academic archaeologist to survey the area and submit a report to a university library
options.C: Pay a fee to the state government heritage fund as compensation for any cultural heritage impacts, which provides automatic clearance for all ground disturbance
options.D: Conduct an Aboriginal Heritage Survey with the participation of the relevant Registered Native Title Body Corporate or Traditional Owner groups, obtain a Heritage Agreement or cultural heritage management plan approved by the relevant authority, and obtain permission before disturbing any identified sites — with ongoing monitoring by Traditional Owners during ground-disturbing works

explanation: Australian heritage protection legislation — including the Aboriginal Heritage Act (various state versions), the Native Title Act 1993, and the EPBC Act (for national heritage) — establishes one of the world's most developed frameworks for Indigenous Cultural Heritage protection, often used as a model. Key requirements include: (1) Heritage survey with Traditional Owner participation — surveys must be conducted with Traditional Owners (not just by academic consultants on their behalf), typically through Walking Country surveys where Traditional Owners physically inspect the ground; (2) Registered heritage body — engagement must be with the legally recognised representatives of the Traditional Owner group, determined through the native title process; (3) Approved Cultural Heritage Management Plan (CHMP) — for significant impacts, a CHMP must be negotiated with Traditional Owners and approved by the relevant authority before any ground disturbance; (4) Ongoing monitoring — Traditional Owner representatives must be present during ground-disturbing activities to identify unexpected sites; (5) Consent for significant impacts — destruction of registered sites typically requires formal ministerial consent, which may be refused. While Australian law is used as the example here, similar frameworks are developing in West African countries under national cultural heritage legislation and FPIC requirements.
key_concept: Indigenous heritage: Traditional Owner surveys on-Country, approved CHMP required before ground disturbance, ongoing monitoring during works
source: Australian Aboriginal Heritage Acts / Native Title Act 1993 / IFC PS8

question: A senior environmental manager at a gold mine in Côte d'Ivoire discovers that monthly water quality discharge reports submitted to the regulator have been falsified by a junior employee to hide exceedances of cyanide effluent standards. The manager reports this to the company's compliance hotline. Under international best practice for whistleblower protection in mining compliance, which protections should apply?

options.A: The manager is not protected because they are in a senior role and are expected to manage compliance issues themselves without escalating
options.B: The manager's report is only protected if submitted to the national environmental regulator directly, not through internal company channels
options.C: The manager should receive confidentiality of identity, protection from retaliation (dismissal, demotion, harassment), an independent investigation of the reported misconduct, and feedback on the outcome — with these protections enshrined in the company's whistleblower policy and ideally in applicable national law
options.D: Whistleblower protections only apply to reports of financial fraud; environmental compliance reports are handled under a separate process without individual protections

explanation: International best practice for whistleblower protection in corporate compliance — as reflected in the UN Convention Against Corruption (UNCAC Art. 33), the G20 High-Level Principles on Whistleblower Protection, the OECD Anti-Bribery Convention Recommendation, and corporate governance codes — establishes that effective whistleblower systems must provide: (1) Confidentiality — the reporter's identity is protected to prevent targeted retaliation; (2) Multiple reporting channels — internal (hotline, ombudsperson, ethics committee) and external (regulator, auditor) options; (3) Non-retaliation — prohibition on dismissal, demotion, harassment, reassignment, or any adverse action against the reporter; (4) Independent investigation — reported misconduct is investigated by parties independent of those implicated; (5) Feedback — the reporter receives appropriate communication about investigation outcomes; (6) No jurisdictional limits on subject matter — protections apply to all categories of misconduct, not just financial fraud. In mining contexts, environmental falsification is a serious compliance matter that can trigger regulatory sanctions, permit revocation, and criminal liability for both the company and individuals. The 2022 EU Whistleblower Protection Directive and equivalent laws in Australia, Canada, and the UK extend these protections broadly. Effective implementation also requires training all employees on reporting rights and a demonstrated culture of non-retaliation from senior leadership.
key_concept: Whistleblower: confidentiality, non-retaliation, independent investigation, feedback — applies to all misconduct categories including environmental falsification
source: UNCAC Art. 33 / OECD Anti-Bribery Convention / G20 Whistleblower Principles / EU Directive 2019/1937
